ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yaki beef udon; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Udon noodles — used as the primary starchy component anchor; dish-level FoodEx2 code for yaki beef udon not available in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers udon noodles as a food item; no composite dish-level code for yaki beef udon exists. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for yaki beef udon or equivalent Japanese noodle dish composite.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of yaki beef udon with dashi broth weighs approximately 600 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu for this item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Udon noodles cooked (SR Legacy #20109) and Beef stir-fried composite; values weighted and blended for dish composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for yaki beef udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Per-100g values were derived by blending USDA SR Legacy data for cooked udon noodles (~55% by weight), lean beef stir-fried (~20%), dashi broth (~20%), and minor condiments (soy sauce, mirin, spring onions, mushrooms ~5%). The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived blend and found broadly consistent; USDA-anchored values are used as the authoritative basis. Input values retained where within ±10% of USDA blend; adjusted where divergent.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles (GI ~55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for yaki beef udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain cooked udon noodles (GI ~55), which constitute the dominant carbohydrate source (~55% by weight). The beef, broth, and condiment components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 79.8) / 100 = 43.9, where carbohydrate per 600g serving = 13.3 × 6 = 79.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g yields approximately 1800 mg sodium per 600g serving — 78% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and a lighter dashi broth concentration, and avoid adding extra condiments, to keep the per-serving sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat udon noodles have a GI of approximately 55 (low) but the 600g serving delivers ~79.8g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~43.9, which is very high and will produce a significant postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200g, increase the vegetable content, and pair the dish with a protein-rich side to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.3g/100g (7.8g per serving) and sodium at 300mg/100g (1800mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; trace trans fat (0.08g/100g, ~0.5g per serving) from beef adds marginal additional risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a bowl, request leaner beef cuts or substitute with chicken breast, and ask for reduced-sodium broth to keep saturated fat below 5g and sodium below 900mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat with high arachidonic acid (omega-6) content that promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; trace trans fat from beef adds marginal additional inflammatory burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with salmon or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid load, and retain shiitake mushrooms and any leafy greens for their anti-inflammatory micronutrient contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yaki Beef Udon with Dashi Broth (焼き牛うどん だし汁入り) is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-flour udon noodles served in a savoury umami-rich dashi broth — typically made from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (bonito flakes) — and topped with tender grilled or stir-fried sliced beef. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, and commonly garnished with spring onions and shiitake mushrooms. The udon noodles provide the dominant carbohydrate base (approximately 13.3g carbohydrate per 100g), while the beef contributes protein (approximately 5.8g per 100g) and fat (approximately 3.3g per 100g). The dashi broth is the primary source of sodium (approximately 300mg per 100g), making the full 600g serving sodium-dense at approximately 1800mg. The dish has a low glycemic index of approximately 55 driven by the refined wheat udon noodles, but the large serving size produces a very high glycemic load (~43.9 per bowl). Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, high concern for type 2 diabetes due to glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium. It is not suitable for gluten-free diets. This dish is commonly served in Japanese restaurants and food courts in Singapore, and is listed on the GrabFood platform as item 605D.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for yaki beef udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Per-100g values were estimated by blending USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked udon noodles, lean stir-fried beef, dashi broth, and condiments in proportions consistent with the dish description. The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with the USDA-derived blend. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain udon noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Per-100g nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component blend (cooked udon noodles ~55%, lean beef stir-fried ~20%, dashi broth ~20%, condiments ~5%) because no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Input ai_generated values were cross-checked and retained where within ±10% of the USDA blend. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Japanese noodle dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level data exist but dish-level composite values cannot be reliably attributed without a validated recipe weight. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain cooked udon noodles; no direct GI measurement for yaki beef udon with dashi broth exists in any approved GI source. 4. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Sushi', 'Tempura', 'Grilled Fish') are not applicable to this dish and have been removed. 5. The dish is not certified halal; dashi typically contains bonito (fish) and the halal status of the beef is unverified for this GrabFood listing. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 ('noodle dish') used as broader ancestor with match_type 'closest_parent'; FoodEx2 A0FJP (udon noodles) used as closest parent component anchor with match_type 'closest_parent'; no SNOMED CT code found.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Identified that input ethnic_tags (Sushi, Tempura, Grilled Fish) were inapplicable and removed them. Added Japanese local name in native script. Built full USDA-blended nutrient profile. Added GI from University of Sydney analogous estimate. Added all three required health_context conditions with Type 2 Diabetes as machine key. Populated ontology with honest gap notes. Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language. Verified aliases consistency (q8 self-check). Documented all -1 fields in notes (g3). Verified no zero macros requiring justification (g4 — all macros non-zero). Confirmed no forbidden sources used (g2).

Iteration 2: Addressed REJECT reasons from previous validation. Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type to 'closest_parent' and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'adapted' to conform to allowed values. Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi, and updated aliases and aliases_by_language accordingly to satisfy G6, Q1, and Q8. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 55. Verified all aliases in aliases_by_language are present in the flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Addressed REJECT reason from iteration 2 validation — g5_ontology_honesty failure because foodex2_match_type value 'adapted' is not in the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Changed foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' and updated foodex2_gap_note accordingly. Updated notes field to reflect 'closest_parent' match type for FoodEx2. All other sections preserved unchanged.
